1:
"And Moses with the elders of Israel commanded the people, saying, Keep all the commandments which I command you this day." The witnesses note Moses and the elders charging the people to keep all the commandments. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses, with the elders of Israel, commanded the people. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set this as part of the conclusion and renewal of the covenant in the land of Moab. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses with the elders — the united charge; the commanded the people — the leaders pressing the duty; the keep all the commandments — obedience the great business; the the covenant renewed — the people bound to obey (Heb. 13:17).

The witnesses agree the chapter opens with a solemn, united charge to obedience as the covenant was renewed: Moses joined the elders of Israel in commanding the people to "keep all the commandments," the leaders together pressing the duty; the joining of the elders shows the whole authority of the nation set behind the law, and the renewal of the covenant on the eve of entering the land binds the people to obey; it teaches that the keeping of God's commandments is the great business of his people, urged with the full weight of those he has set over them (cf. Heb. 13:17).

So the witnesses make the verse keep all the commandments: The witnesses note the Moses with the elders: the united charge. The witnesses note the commanded the people: the leaders pressing the duty. The witnesses note the keep all the commandments: obedience the great business. The witnesses note the the covenant renewed: the people bound to obey (Heb. 13:17). The expositors observe the Moses with the elders: the united charge. The expositors observe the commanded the people: the leaders pressing the duty. The expositors observe the keep all the commandments: obedience the great business. The expositors observe the the covenant renewed: the people bound to obey (Heb. 13:17). The believer is taught to mark the Moses with the elders, the commanded the people, the keep all the commandments, the the covenant renewed, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that Moses and the elders charged the people to keep all the commandments — a united, solemn call to obedience as the covenant was renewed, the keeping of God's law the great business of his people (Heb. 13:17).
2:
"And it shall be on the day when ye shall pass over Jordan unto the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee, that thou shalt set thee up great stones, and plaister them with plaister:" The witnesses note the command to set up great plastered stones soon after crossing Jordan. The witnesses note that Gill notes "on the day" means about that time, soon after they passed, though not executed until after Ai was destroyed. The witnesses note that the great stones were to be plastered. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the pass over Jordan — entering the land; the set up great stones — soon after crossing; the plaister them — prepared for the law; the God's word first — the law established at the entrance (Josh. 8:30).

The witnesses agree the setting-up of the plastered stones was to be one of Israel's first acts in the land: soon after crossing Jordan, great stones were to be raised and plastered, prepared to receive the law; the prompt erection of this monument shows that the first concern of the people, on entering their inheritance, was to be the public owning of God's word, the law set up in the land before all else; it teaches that God's commandments are to have the first and chief place wherever his people dwell, the word of God established at the very entrance of their life in the land (cf. Josh. 8:30-32).

So the witnesses make the verse set up great stones: The witnesses note the pass over Jordan: entering the land. The witnesses note the set up great stones: soon after crossing. The witnesses note the plaister them: prepared for the law. The witnesses note the God's word first: the law established at the entrance (Josh. 8:30). The expositors observe the pass over Jordan: entering the land. The expositors observe the set up great stones: soon after crossing. The expositors observe the plaister them: prepared for the law. The expositors observe the God's word first: the law established at the entrance (Josh. 8:30). The believer is taught to mark the pass over Jordan, the set up great stones, the plaister them, the God's word first, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that great plastered stones were to be set up soon after crossing Jordan — the public owning of God's word among Israel's first acts in the land, his commandments given the first place (Josh. 8:30-32).
3:
"And thou shalt write upon them all the words of this law, when thou art passed over, that thou mayest go in unto the land... a land that floweth with milk and honey;" The witnesses note the law written on the stones — its words made public in the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was not the whole book of Deuteronomy but the laws of God, perhaps a summary, or the ten commandments; Josephus thought the blessings and curses. The witnesses note that it was written when they passed into the land of milk and honey. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the write all the words — the law made public; the upon the stones — an abiding witness; the when thou art passed over — at the entrance of the land; the the word displayed — that he may run that readeth (Hab. 2:2).

The witnesses agree the writing of the law on the stones made God's word a public and abiding witness in the land: whether the whole law, a summary, or the ten commandments, the words were "written upon them" for all to read, set up where the people entered their inheritance; the publishing of the law shows that God would have his commandments known and owned by all, not hidden but openly displayed; it teaches that the word of God is to be set before his people plainly, "that he may run that readeth it" (Hab. 2:2), the law a standing testimony in the land of promise.

So the witnesses make the verse write the law: The witnesses note the write all the words: the law made public. The witnesses note the upon the stones: an abiding witness. The witnesses note the when thou art passed over: at the entrance of the land. The witnesses note the the word displayed: that he may run that readeth (Hab. 2:2). The expositors observe the write all the words: the law made public. The expositors observe the upon the stones: an abiding witness. The expositors observe the when thou art passed over: at the entrance of the land. The expositors observe the the word displayed: that he may run that readeth (Hab. 2:2). The believer is taught to mark the write all the words, the upon the stones, the when thou art passed over, the the word displayed, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that the law was written on the stones, made public in the land — God's word an abiding, open witness, his commandments set plainly before his people (Hab. 2:2).
4:
"Therefore it shall be when ye be gone over Jordan, that ye shall set up these stones, which I command you this day, in mount Ebal, and thou shalt plaister them with plaister." The witnesses note the stones to be set up specifically on Mount Ebal — the mount of cursing. The witnesses note that Gill notes some time after they had passed Jordan. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the stones to be raised on Mount Ebal and plastered. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the set up the stones — the written law; the in mount Ebal — the mount of cursing; the plaister them — prepared for the words; the the law condemns — blessing sought beyond it (Gal. 3:10).

The witnesses agree it is significant that the law was set up on Ebal, the mount of cursing rather than Gerizim, the mount of blessing: the written law stood on the very mountain from which the curses were pronounced, a fitting place, for the law in itself, broken by sinners, brings a curse and not a blessing; the law written on Ebal teaches that the commandment, holy as it is, condemns the transgressor, and that blessing must be sought beyond the bare law — a quiet pointing, even here, to the need of a Saviour from the curse the law pronounces (cf. Gal. 3:10).

So the witnesses make the verse the law on Ebal: The witnesses note the set up the stones: the written law. The witnesses note the in mount Ebal: the mount of cursing. The witnesses note the plaister them: prepared for the words. The witnesses note the the law condemns: blessing sought beyond it (Gal. 3:10). The expositors observe the set up the stones: the written law. The expositors observe the in mount Ebal: the mount of cursing. The expositors observe the plaister them: prepared for the words. The expositors observe the the law condemns: blessing sought beyond it (Gal. 3:10). The believer is taught to mark the set up the stones, the in mount Ebal, the plaister them, the the law condemns, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that the law was set up on Ebal, the mount of cursing — fitting, for the law broken brings a curse, blessing to be sought beyond the bare law, a pointing to the Saviour from its curse (Gal. 3:10).
5:
"And there shalt thou build an altar unto the LORD thy God, an altar of stones: thou shalt not lift up any iron tool upon them." The witnesses note an altar to be built on the same mount, of stones untouched by any iron tool. The witnesses note that Gill notes the altar was on the same mountain, though not of the same stones; Joshua so built it. The witnesses note that no iron tool was to be lifted upon the stones. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the build an altar — on the mount of cursing; the of stones — for sacrifice and atonement; the no iron tool — unhewn, untouched by man's work; the grace, not works — the way not by merit (Exod. 20:25).

The witnesses agree the unhewn altar, beside the condemning law, brought in the way of grace: on the very mount of cursing an altar was built, where sacrifice might be offered and atonement made; and it was of stones "not lift up any iron tool upon them" — unhewn, untouched by human workmanship; the rule, given before (Exod. 20:25), teaches that the way of approach to God is not by man's labour or merit but by his appointed sacrifice, the altar of grace set against the law of works; that, where the law curses, the altar — pointing to Christ — provides a way of blessing, salvation not of works lest any boast.

So the witnesses make the verse the unhewn altar: The witnesses note the build an altar: on the mount of cursing. The witnesses note the of stones: for sacrifice and atonement. The witnesses note the no iron tool: unhewn, untouched by man's work. The witnesses note the grace, not works: the way not by merit (Exod. 20:25). The expositors observe the build an altar: on the mount of cursing. The expositors observe the of stones: for sacrifice and atonement. The expositors observe the no iron tool: unhewn, untouched by man's work. The expositors observe the grace, not works: the way not by merit (Exod. 20:25). The believer is taught to mark the build an altar, the of stones, the no iron tool, the grace, not works, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that an unhewn altar was built on the mount of cursing — the way of grace beside the condemning law, approach to God not by man's work but by appointed sacrifice, the altar pointing to Christ (Exod. 20:25).
6:
"Thou shalt build the altar of the LORD thy God of whole stones; and thou shalt offer burnt offerings thereon unto the LORD thy God:" The witnesses note the altar of whole stones, on which burnt offerings were to be made. The witnesses note that Gill notes the altar was built of whole stones, as Joshua did. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the idea of the burnt offering is the dedication of man's life to God; Wesley notes the whole, unhewn stones, the law written on them and the altar joined. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the altar of whole stones — the way of grace; the burnt offerings — wholly consumed; the dedication to God — man's life given up; the Christ offered wholly — the entire devotion that accepts us (Rom. 12:1).

The witnesses agree the altar of whole stones and its burnt offerings set forth man's entire dedication to God by sacrifice: the unhewn stones marked the way of grace, and on them the burnt offering — wholly consumed — figured "the dedication of man's life to God" (Ellicott); the joining of the law written and the altar built shows that the demand of the law and the provision of sacrifice stand together, the one condemning and the other atoning; the burnt offering points to Christ, who offered himself wholly to God, that those who could not keep the law might be accepted in his entire devotion (cf. Rom. 12:1).

So the witnesses make the verse burnt offerings: The witnesses note the the altar of whole stones: the way of grace. The witnesses note the burnt offerings: wholly consumed. The witnesses note the dedication to God: man's life given up. The witnesses note the Christ offered wholly: the entire devotion that accepts us (Rom. 12:1). The expositors observe the the altar of whole stones: the way of grace. The expositors observe the burnt offerings: wholly consumed. The expositors observe the dedication to God: man's life given up. The expositors observe the Christ offered wholly: the entire devotion that accepts us (Rom. 12:1). The believer is taught to mark the the altar of whole stones, the burnt offerings, the dedication to God, the Christ offered wholly, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that burnt offerings were made on the altar of whole stones — figuring man's entire dedication to God, the law and the altar together, pointing to Christ who offered himself wholly that we be accepted (Rom. 12:1).
7:
"And thou shalt offer peace offerings, and shalt eat there, and rejoice before the LORD thy God." The witnesses note peace offerings made, and the people eating and rejoicing before the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the peace offering — part to God, part to the priest, and the rest to the owner, eaten with his friends. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes these were offerings for health and peace. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the peace offerings — shared between God, priest, and owner; the eat there — a meal of fellowship; the rejoice before the LORD — reconciliation and gladness; the Christ our peace — communion through the atonement (Eph. 2:14).

The witnesses agree the peace offerings and the feast that followed set forth reconciliation and joyful communion with God: part of the offering was God's, part the priest's, and the rest eaten by the worshipper "with his friends," a shared meal of fellowship; the rejoicing before the LORD shows that the end of sacrifice is peace and gladness, the reconciled enjoying communion with God; the peace offering points to Christ, "our peace" (Eph. 2:14), through whom the redeemed have fellowship with God and one another, the feast of peace following the atonement.

So the witnesses make the verse peace offerings and joy: The witnesses note the peace offerings: shared between God, priest, and owner. The witnesses note the eat there: a meal of fellowship. The witnesses note the rejoice before the LORD: reconciliation and gladness. The witnesses note the Christ our peace: communion through the atonement (Eph. 2:14). The expositors observe the peace offerings: shared between God, priest, and owner. The expositors observe the eat there: a meal of fellowship. The expositors observe the rejoice before the LORD: reconciliation and gladness. The expositors observe the Christ our peace: communion through the atonement (Eph. 2:14). The believer is taught to mark the peace offerings, the eat there, the rejoice before the LORD, the Christ our peace, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that peace offerings were made and the people rejoiced before the LORD — reconciliation and joyful communion the end of sacrifice, pointing to Christ our peace, the feast following the atonement (Eph. 2:14).
8:
"And thou shalt write upon the stones all the words of this law very plainly." The witnesses note the law written very plainly on the stones, that all might read it. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was written on the first stones set up before, on which the words were inscribed before the altar was erected. The witnesses note that it was to be written "very plainly." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the write all the words — the law inscribed; the upon the stones — for all to read; the very plainly — clear and accessible; the none excused — the word not hidden (Deut. 30:11).

The witnesses agree the charge that the law be written "very plainly" presses the clearness and accessibility of God's word: the words were to be inscribed so distinctly that all could read them, none excused by obscurity; the plainness of the writing shows that God would have his law understood by all his people, not veiled or hard, but open to every reader; it teaches that the word of God is to be set forth clearly, that the simplest may know his will, the law made plain "that he may run that readeth it," and the people left without excuse for ignorance (cf. Deut. 30:11, this commandment... is not hidden from thee).

So the witnesses make the verse very plainly: The witnesses note the write all the words: the law inscribed. The witnesses note the upon the stones: for all to read. The witnesses note the very plainly: clear and accessible. The witnesses note the none excused: the word not hidden (Deut. 30:11). The expositors observe the write all the words: the law inscribed. The expositors observe the upon the stones: for all to read. The expositors observe the very plainly: clear and accessible. The expositors observe the none excused: the word not hidden (Deut. 30:11). The believer is taught to mark the write all the words, the upon the stones, the very plainly, the none excused, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that the law was written very plainly on the stones — God's word made clear and accessible to all, the simplest able to know his will, none excused by obscurity (Deut. 30:11).
9:
"And Moses and the priests the Levites spake unto all Israel, saying, Take heed, and hearken, O Israel; this day thou art become the people of the LORD thy God." The witnesses note Moses and the priests declaring that Israel, in renewing the covenant, had this day become the people of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the priests who were Levites joined with Moses in the exhortation. The witnesses note that this day they were become the people of the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses and the priests — the joint exhortation; the take heed and hearken — the solemn call; the this day — the covenant renewed; the become his people — a fresh consecration (2 Cor. 8:5).

The witnesses agree the covenant-renewal made the people, afresh, the LORD's own: "this day thou art become the people of the LORD thy God," their solemn re-engagement reckoned as a fresh becoming his; the word shows that the covenant is not a thing of the past only but renewed and owned anew by each generation, the people taking up again their relation to God; it teaches that the bond between God and his people is to be deliberately renewed and entered, "this day" a fresh consecration, the privilege of belonging to him claimed and confessed (cf. 2 Cor. 8:5, they... first gave their own selves to the Lord).

So the witnesses make the verse become the LORD's people: The witnesses note the Moses and the priests: the joint exhortation. The witnesses note the take heed and hearken: the solemn call. The witnesses note the this day: the covenant renewed. The witnesses note the become his people: a fresh consecration (2 Cor. 8:5). The expositors observe the Moses and the priests: the joint exhortation. The expositors observe the take heed and hearken: the solemn call. The expositors observe the this day: the covenant renewed. The expositors observe the become his people: a fresh consecration (2 Cor. 8:5). The believer is taught to mark the Moses and the priests, the take heed and hearken, the this day, the become his people, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that Moses and the priests declared Israel this day become the people of the LORD — the covenant renewed and owned anew, a fresh consecration, the bond with God deliberately entered (2 Cor. 8:5).
10:
"Thou shalt therefore obey the voice of the LORD thy God, and do his commandments and his statutes, which I command thee this day." The witnesses note the duty drawn from their being his people — therefore obey the voice of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes obeying whatever he directs in his word, by his prophets, and especially by his Son, eminently called the Word of the Lord. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the charge to obey and do his commandments. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the therefore obey — the duty from the privilege; the the voice of the LORD — heard in his word; the do his commandments — the people proving their belonging; the especially by his Son — hear ye him (Matt. 17:5).

The witnesses agree the "therefore" binds obedience to their privilege: because they were now become the people of the LORD, they were to "obey the voice of the LORD" and do his commandments; the duty flows from the relation — those who belong to God are bound to hearken to him; Gill marks that this obedience reaches to all God speaks in his word and by his prophets, "and especially by his Son," the Word of the Lord; it teaches that the people of God prove their belonging by obedience, hearing his voice in his word and supremely in Christ, "hear ye him" (Matt. 17:5).

So the witnesses make the verse therefore obey: The witnesses note the therefore obey: the duty from the privilege. The witnesses note the the voice of the LORD: heard in his word. The witnesses note the do his commandments: the people proving their belonging. The witnesses note the especially by his Son: hear ye him (Matt. 17:5). The expositors observe the therefore obey: the duty from the privilege. The expositors observe the the voice of the LORD: heard in his word. The expositors observe the do his commandments: the people proving their belonging. The expositors observe the especially by his Son: hear ye him (Matt. 17:5). The believer is taught to mark the therefore obey, the the voice of the LORD, the do his commandments, the especially by his Son, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that being his people, they were therefore to obey the voice of the LORD — obedience flowing from belonging, hearing him in his word and supremely in Christ his Son, hear ye him (Matt. 17:5).
11:
"And Moses charged the people the same day, saying," The witnesses note Moses, the same day, charging the people concerning the blessing and the cursing from the mounts. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses gave the orders to set up stones, write the law, build the altar, and offer sacrifices when come into the land. The witnesses note that Henry marks that when the law was written to be seen and read by all, the people were to be solemnly called to it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the charged the people — concerning blessing and cursing; the the same day — with earnestness; the the law pressed home — laid upon the conscience; the seen and felt — the word brought home (Neh. 8:8).

The puritan expositor (Henry) marks the fitness of joining the public reading of the law to a solemn calling of the people to it: the law written and set up was now to be enforced upon their consciences by the great ceremony of blessing and cursing from the mounts; the same day's charge shows the earnestness with which God would have his word pressed home, not merely displayed but laid upon the heart with all solemnity; it teaches that the word read is to be brought home to the conscience, the people summoned to hear and answer to it, that the law be not only seen but felt (cf. Neh. 8:8, they read... and gave the sense).

So the witnesses make the verse charged the same day: The witnesses note the charged the people: concerning blessing and cursing. The witnesses note the the same day: with earnestness. The witnesses note the the law pressed home: laid upon the conscience. The witnesses note the seen and felt: the word brought home (Neh. 8:8). The expositors observe the charged the people: concerning blessing and cursing. The expositors observe the the same day: with earnestness. The expositors observe the the law pressed home: laid upon the conscience. The expositors observe the seen and felt: the word brought home (Neh. 8:8). The believer is taught to mark the charged the people, the the same day, the the law pressed home, the seen and felt, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that Moses charged the people the same day concerning the blessing and cursing — the law not merely displayed but pressed home upon the conscience, the word brought home to the heart (Neh. 8:8).
12:
"These shall stand upon mount Gerizim to bless the people, when ye are come over Jordan; Simeon, and Levi, and Judah, and Issachar, and Joseph, and Benjamin:" The witnesses note six tribes appointed to stand on Mount Gerizim to bless the people. The witnesses note that Gill notes these tribes were to stand on Gerizim to bless. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch name the six tribes for blessing on Gerizim. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the upon mount Gerizim — the mount of blessing; the to bless the people — six tribes appointed; the the nation engaged — all Israel a party to the covenant; the the two ways — blessing and cursing set before them (Deut. 30:19).

The witnesses agree the dividing of the tribes upon the two mounts made the whole nation, as it were, parties to the covenant of blessing and cursing: six tribes stood on Gerizim "to bless the people," the mount of fruitfulness, while six stood on Ebal to curse; the solemn arrangement engaged all Israel in the great transaction, the blessing and the curse set before the assembled nation; it pictures the two ways ever set before the people of God — life and death, blessing and curse — that they choose the blessing by obedience, the whole congregation made to hear and own both (cf. Deut. 30:19, I have set before you life and death, blessing and cursing).

So the witnesses make the verse tribes for blessing: The witnesses note the upon mount Gerizim: the mount of blessing. The witnesses note the to bless the people: six tribes appointed. The witnesses note the the nation engaged: all Israel a party to the covenant. The witnesses note the the two ways: blessing and cursing set before them (Deut. 30:19). The expositors observe the upon mount Gerizim: the mount of blessing. The expositors observe the to bless the people: six tribes appointed. The expositors observe the the nation engaged: all Israel a party to the covenant. The expositors observe the the two ways: blessing and cursing set before them (Deut. 30:19). The believer is taught to mark the upon mount Gerizim, the to bless the people, the the nation engaged, the the two ways, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that six tribes stood on Gerizim to bless — the whole nation engaged in the covenant, the two ways of blessing and cursing set before the assembled people (Deut. 30:19).
13:
"And these shall stand upon mount Ebal to curse; Reuben, Gad, and Asher, and Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali." The witnesses note the other six tribes appointed to stand on Mount Ebal to pronounce the curse. The witnesses note that Gill notes Ebal was dry and rocky, barren, like the earth that bears thorns and is nigh unto cursing — a fit place to curse and emblem of those to be cursed. The witnesses note that Wesley sets these tribes for the curse. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the upon mount Ebal — the barren mount of cursing; the to curse — six tribes appointed; the an emblem of the cursed — the ground nigh unto cursing (Heb. 6:8); the the law's curse — for the transgressor, to be fled.

The witnesses agree the choice of Ebal for the curse was full of meaning: the barren, rocky mount, like the ground that "bears briers and thorns... is nigh unto cursing" (cf. Heb. 6:8), was the fit place from which to pronounce the curse, a very emblem of the cursed estate of the disobedient; the setting of six tribes there to curse shows that the law has its curse for the transgressor as surely as its blessing for the obedient; it teaches the solemn truth that under the law lies a curse, which the sinner must own and from which he must flee to the grace that the altar on the same mount foreshadows.

So the witnesses make the verse tribes for cursing: The witnesses note the upon mount Ebal: the barren mount of cursing. The witnesses note the to curse: six tribes appointed. The witnesses note the an emblem of the cursed: the ground nigh unto cursing (Heb. 6:8). The witnesses note the the law's curse: for the transgressor, to be fled. The expositors observe the upon mount Ebal: the barren mount of cursing. The expositors observe the to curse: six tribes appointed. The expositors observe the an emblem of the cursed: the ground nigh unto cursing (Heb. 6:8). The expositors observe the the law's curse: for the transgressor, to be fled. The believer is taught to mark the upon mount Ebal, the to curse, the an emblem of the cursed, the the law's curse, and to flee to the grace that answers the law. The verse thus records that six tribes stood on Ebal to curse — the barren mount a fit emblem of the cursed estate of the disobedient, the law's curse for the transgressor, from which the sinner must flee to grace (Heb. 6:8).
14:
"And the Levites shall speak, and say unto all the men of Israel with a loud voice," The witnesses note the Levites in the valley pronouncing the blessings and curses aloud to all Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes not the whole tribe (which stood on Gerizim) but the priests placed in the valley between the mounts, who pronounced both blessings and curses in the hearing of all. The witnesses note that they spake with a loud voice. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Levites shall speak — the priests in the valley; the unto all Israel — that all might hear; the with a loud voice — the public proclamation; the the law declared — the priests ministering the word (Mal. 2:7).

The witnesses agree the Levites in the valley gave voice to the great covenant transaction: standing between the mounts, the priests pronounced "with a loud voice" both blessing and curse, that all Israel might hear and answer; the public, audible proclamation engaged the whole assembly, none able to plead ignorance; it shows that God would have his law, with its sanctions of blessing and curse, openly declared and brought home to every ear, the priests as the ministers of his word setting it solemnly before the people (cf. Mal. 2:7, the priest's lips should keep knowledge).

So the witnesses make the verse the Levites speak: The witnesses note the the Levites shall speak: the priests in the valley. The witnesses note the unto all Israel: that all might hear. The witnesses note the with a loud voice: the public proclamation. The witnesses note the the law declared: the priests ministering the word (Mal. 2:7). The expositors observe the the Levites shall speak: the priests in the valley. The expositors observe the unto all Israel: that all might hear. The expositors observe the with a loud voice: the public proclamation. The expositors observe the the law declared: the priests ministering the word (Mal. 2:7). The believer is taught to mark the the Levites shall speak, the unto all Israel, the with a loud voice, the the law declared, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that the Levites pronounced the blessings and curses aloud to all Israel — the covenant openly declared, the priests setting God's law and its sanctions solemnly before every ear (Mal. 2:7).
15:
"Cursed be the man that maketh any graven or molten image, an abomination unto the LORD... and putteth it in a secret place. And all the people shall answer and say, Amen." The witnesses note the first curse — on the maker of idols, even in secret — and the people's "Amen." The witnesses note that Gill notes the blessings and their form are not recorded, to put men upon seeking them elsewhere, only to be had in Christ; the curse falls on secret idolatry. The witnesses note that all the people answered, Amen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a graven image — the maker of idols; the in a secret place — god seeing the hidden; the the blessings unrecorded — sought only in Christ (Gill); the all say Amen — owning the justice of the curse (Gal. 3:10).

The witnesses agree the curses begin where the law begins — with idolatry, even hidden idolatry — and the people's "Amen" owns the justice of the sentence: cursed is the maker of a graven image set "in a secret place," for God sees the hidden sin; Gill marks the striking silence as to the blessings — they are "not recorded" here, that men might seek them elsewhere, for blessing comes not from the law but only in Christ; the curse on secret idolatry teaches that God judges the heart's hidden worship, and the people's "Amen" binds them to the righteousness of his judgment (cf. Gal. 3:10).

So the witnesses make the verse cursed the idol-maker: The witnesses note the a graven image: the maker of idols. The witnesses note the in a secret place: God seeing the hidden. The witnesses note the the blessings unrecorded: sought only in Christ (Gill). The witnesses note the all say Amen: owning the justice of the curse (Gal. 3:10). The expositors observe the a graven image: the maker of idols. The expositors observe the in a secret place: God seeing the hidden. The expositors observe the the blessings unrecorded: sought only in Christ (Gill). The expositors observe the all say Amen: owning the justice of the curse (Gal. 3:10). The believer is taught to mark the a graven image, the in a secret place, the the blessings unrecorded, the all say Amen, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is the maker of secret idols, the people saying Amen — God judging the hidden heart, the blessings unrecorded that they be sought in Christ, the people owning the justice of the curse (Gal. 3:10).
16:
"Cursed be he that setteth light by his father or his mother. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the curse on him who secretly despises and dishonours his parents. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is of one who secretly despises them in his heart, mocking by looks and gestures unseen by others; open cursing was punishable by the magistrate with death. The witnesses note that all the people said, Amen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the setteth light by — secretly despising; the his father or mother — the honour withheld; the hidden contempt — beyond the magistrate's reach; the the heart searched — god curses the secret slight (Prov. 30:17).

The witnesses agree the curse reaches the inward and secret dishonouring of parents, beyond the magistrate's reach: not the open cursing only — punishable by death — but the heart that "setteth light by" father and mother, the private contempt shown by look and gesture, falls under God's curse; the law thus searches the secret slighting of the honour due to parents, which men cannot punish but God sees; it teaches that the fifth commandment binds the heart as well as the hand, and that hidden contempt of parents is no small sin but one God himself curses (cf. Prov. 30:17, the eye that mocketh at his father).

So the witnesses make the verse cursed who slights parents: The witnesses note the setteth light by: secretly despising. The witnesses note the his father or mother: the honour withheld. The witnesses note the hidden contempt: beyond the magistrate's reach. The witnesses note the the heart searched: God curses the secret slight (Prov. 30:17). The expositors observe the setteth light by: secretly despising. The expositors observe the his father or mother: the honour withheld. The expositors observe the hidden contempt: beyond the magistrate's reach. The expositors observe the the heart searched: God curses the secret slight (Prov. 30:17). The believer is taught to mark the setteth light by, the his father or mother, the hidden contempt, the the heart searched, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who secretly slights his parents — the law searching the hidden contempt beyond man's reach, the fifth commandment binding the heart, God himself cursing the secret dishonour (Prov. 30:17).
17:
"Cursed be he that removeth his neighbour's landmark. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the curse on him who secretly moves his neighbour's boundary to steal his land. The witnesses note that Gill, with Jarchi, notes the removing of the landmark backward to steal ground, commonly done secretly. The witnesses note that Clarke refers to the earlier law (Deut. 19:14). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the removeth the landmark — moving the boundary; the his neighbour's — to steal his ground; the done secretly — unseen by men; the seen by God — the hidden fraud cursed (Prov. 22:28).

The witnesses agree the curse falls on the secret fraud of stealing land by moving the boundary: the landmark "removed backward" to filch a neighbour's ground was a theft done quietly, unseen by men, yet seen by God; the law brings under its curse this hidden dishonesty, which the courts might never detect; it teaches that God guards the rights of property and the honesty of neighbours, and that secret fraud, however undetected by men, is open before him and falls under his judgment, "remove not the ancient landmark" (Prov. 22:28), the hidden thief cursed by God.

So the witnesses make the verse cursed who steals land: The witnesses note the removeth the landmark: moving the boundary. The witnesses note the his neighbour's: to steal his ground. The witnesses note the done secretly: unseen by men. The witnesses note the seen by God: the hidden fraud cursed (Prov. 22:28). The expositors observe the removeth the landmark: moving the boundary. The expositors observe the his neighbour's: to steal his ground. The expositors observe the done secretly: unseen by men. The expositors observe the seen by God: the hidden fraud cursed (Prov. 22:28). The believer is taught to mark the removeth the landmark, the his neighbour's, the done secretly, the seen by God, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who secretly moves his neighbour's landmark — the hidden fraud of stealing land, unseen by men but open before God, the secret thief falling under his curse (Prov. 22:28).
18:
"Cursed be he that maketh the blind to wander out of the way. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the curse on him who misleads the blind or any who trusts his guidance. The witnesses note that Gill notes the misdirecting of the blind knowingly, or laying a stumbling block; the Targum extends it to misleading a traveller who, like a blind man, knows not his way. The witnesses note that Jarchi takes it of blinding any in a matter, giving evil counsel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the blind to wander — misleading the helpless; the out of the way — by false guidance or counsel; the a betrayal of trust — abusing dependence; the the helpless guarded — no stumblingblock before the blind (Lev. 19:14).

The witnesses agree the curse falls on the cruel betrayal of trust — misleading the helpless: to make "the blind to wander out of the way," whether literally, or by deceiving the ignorant traveller, or by giving false counsel to one who trusts our guidance, is a base wickedness God curses; the law thus guards the helpless from those who would abuse their dependence, branding as cursed the abuse of another's blindness or ignorance; it teaches the sacred duty of dealing truly with those who rely on us, and that to lead the trusting astray, for gain or malice, is a sin open before God who pities the helpless (cf. Lev. 19:14, nor put a stumblingblock before the blind).

So the witnesses make the verse cursed who misleads the blind: The witnesses note the the blind to wander: misleading the helpless. The witnesses note the out of the way: by false guidance or counsel. The witnesses note the a betrayal of trust: abusing dependence. The witnesses note the the helpless guarded: no stumblingblock before the blind (Lev. 19:14). The expositors observe the the blind to wander: misleading the helpless. The expositors observe the out of the way: by false guidance or counsel. The expositors observe the a betrayal of trust: abusing dependence. The expositors observe the the helpless guarded: no stumblingblock before the blind (Lev. 19:14). The believer is taught to mark the the blind to wander, the out of the way, the a betrayal of trust, the the helpless guarded, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who misleads the blind — the cruel betrayal of trust, abusing another's dependence, God guarding the helpless and cursing those who lead the trusting astray (Lev. 19:14).
19:
"Cursed be he that perverteth the judgment of the stranger, fatherless, and widow. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the curse on him who perverts justice against the most helpless — stranger, fatherless, and widow. The witnesses note that Gill notes the perverting of the judgment of the stranger, fatherless, and widow. The witnesses note that all the people said, Amen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the perverteth the judgment — of the helpless; the stranger, fatherless, widow — with no patron but God; the injustice under law — doubly hateful to God; the God their avenger — a father of the fatherless (Ps. 68:5).

The witnesses agree the curse falls on the injustice done to those least able to defend themselves: to "pervert the judgment of the stranger, fatherless, and widow" — the very ones with no patron but God — is a wrong he peculiarly abhors and curses; the law thus sets God himself as the avenger of the helpless against corrupt or partial judges; it teaches that the oppression of the weak under colour of law is doubly hateful to God, who is "a father of the fatherless, and a judge of the widows" (Ps. 68:5), and will surely curse those who wrong them.

So the witnesses make the verse cursed who wrongs the helpless: The witnesses note the perverteth the judgment: of the helpless. The witnesses note the stranger, fatherless, widow: with no patron but God. The witnesses note the injustice under law: doubly hateful to God. The witnesses note the God their avenger: a father of the fatherless (Ps. 68:5). The expositors observe the perverteth the judgment: of the helpless. The expositors observe the stranger, fatherless, widow: with no patron but God. The expositors observe the injustice under law: doubly hateful to God. The expositors observe the God their avenger: a father of the fatherless (Ps. 68:5). The believer is taught to mark the perverteth the judgment, the stranger, fatherless, widow, the injustice under law, the God their avenger, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who perverts the judgment of the stranger, fatherless, and widow — God the avenger of the helpless, the oppression of the weak under colour of law doubly hateful to him who is a father of the fatherless (Ps. 68:5).
20:
"Cursed be he that lieth with his father's wife; because he uncovereth his father's skirt. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the curse on incest with the father's wife, as Reuben's sin. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is as Reuben did, forbidden in the law (Lev. 18:8). The witnesses note that all the people said, Amen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the his father's wife — reuben's sin; the uncovereth his father's skirt — defiling the father's bed; the done in secret — hidden from men; the God sees and judges — no secret uncleanness escapes him (Heb. 13:4).

The witnesses agree the curse now falls on the secret sins of incest, beginning with the father's wife: to "lie with his father's wife" — Reuben's ancient sin — was a defiling of the father's bed and a breaking of the bounds of nature, forbidden in the law; these incestuous acts, the witnesses note, are "done in secret," hidden from men, and so are met with God's curse, who sees what men cannot; the law thus guards the purity of the family and brands the hidden defilement of natural bonds, teaching that no secret uncleanness escapes the eye and the judgment of God (cf. Heb. 13:4, whoremongers and adulterers God will judge).

So the witnesses make the verse cursed the incest: The witnesses note the his father's wife: Reuben's sin. The witnesses note the uncovereth his father's skirt: defiling the father's bed. The witnesses note the done in secret: hidden from men. The witnesses note the God sees and judges: no secret uncleanness escapes him (Heb. 13:4). The expositors observe the his father's wife: Reuben's sin. The expositors observe the uncovereth his father's skirt: defiling the father's bed. The expositors observe the done in secret: hidden from men. The expositors observe the God sees and judges: no secret uncleanness escapes him (Heb. 13:4). The believer is taught to mark the his father's wife, the uncovereth his father's skirt, the done in secret, the God sees and judges, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who lies with his father's wife — the secret defilement of the family bed, hidden from men but met with God's curse, no uncleanness escaping his judgment (Heb. 13:4).
21:
"Cursed be he that lieth with any manner of beast. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the curse on bestiality, an unnatural abomination. The witnesses note that Gill refers to the law forbidding it (Lev. 18:23). The witnesses note that all the people said, Amen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the any manner of beast — the unnatural abomination; the a confusion — below the dignity of man; the done in secret — hidden in darkness; the accursed before God — the unclean not held guiltless (Lev. 18:23).

The witnesses agree the curse falls on the unnatural sin of bestiality, a corruption deeper than even incest: to "lie with any manner of beast" is to sink below the dignity of man and to confound the order God set in creation, a thing the law calls confusion; done in secret and hidden from men, it is met with God's curse, who sees the darkest deeds; the law brands this abomination to guard the sanctity of human nature and the bounds of creation, teaching that the corruptions men hide in darkness are open and accursed before God, who will not hold the unclean guiltless (cf. Lev. 18:23, it is confusion).

So the witnesses make the verse cursed the unnatural sin: The witnesses note the any manner of beast: the unnatural abomination. The witnesses note the a confusion: below the dignity of man. The witnesses note the done in secret: hidden in darkness. The witnesses note the accursed before God: the unclean not held guiltless (Lev. 18:23). The expositors observe the any manner of beast: the unnatural abomination. The expositors observe the a confusion: below the dignity of man. The expositors observe the done in secret: hidden in darkness. The expositors observe the accursed before God: the unclean not held guiltless (Lev. 18:23). The believer is taught to mark the any manner of beast, the a confusion, the done in secret, the accursed before God, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who lies with any beast — the unnatural confusion that sinks below man's dignity, hidden in darkness yet accursed before God, who sees the darkest deeds (Lev. 18:23).
22:
"Cursed be he that lieth with his sister, the daughter of his father, or the daughter of his mother. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the curse on incest with a sister, forbidden by the law. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is forbidden in the law (Lev. 18:9). The witnesses note that all the people said, Amen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the lieth with his sister — of father or mother; the the bounds of kinship — broken; the done in secret — hidden from men; the cursed before God — the hidden violation judged (Ezek. 22:11).

The witnesses agree the curse continues over the secret sins of incest, here with a sister: to "lie with his sister" — whether of father or mother — was a breach of the bounds of kinship the law had set, a defiling of the nearest natural bond; like the others, such a sin is "done in secret," beyond the sight or reach of men, and so falls under the curse of God who searches the hidden; the law guards the purity of the family from within, teaching that the closest relations are to be held sacred, and that the secret violation of them is cursed before God, however concealed from human eyes (cf. Ezek. 22:11).

So the witnesses make the verse cursed who defiles his sister: The witnesses note the lieth with his sister: of father or mother. The witnesses note the the bounds of kinship: broken. The witnesses note the done in secret: hidden from men. The witnesses note the cursed before God: the hidden violation judged (Ezek. 22:11). The expositors observe the lieth with his sister: of father or mother. The expositors observe the the bounds of kinship: broken. The expositors observe the done in secret: hidden from men. The expositors observe the cursed before God: the hidden violation judged (Ezek. 22:11). The believer is taught to mark the lieth with his sister, the the bounds of kinship, the done in secret, the cursed before God, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who lies with his sister — the secret breach of the closest natural bond, hidden from men but cursed before God who searches the heart (Ezek. 22:11).
23:
"Cursed be he that lieth with his mother in law. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the curse on incest with a mother-in-law, completing the secret sins of the flesh. The witnesses note that Gill notes this completes the list; all these incestuous or brutal copulations may well be supposed to be done in secret. The witnesses note that all the people said, Amen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the his mother in law — the last of the incest-curses; the a breach of kinship — the bounds broken; the done in secret — hidden from men; the no escape by concealment — every secret thing judged (Eccl. 12:14).

The witnesses agree the last of the incest-curses confirms the running theme — that these are the secret sins of the flesh, hidden from men but open to God: to "lie with his mother in law" was another breach of the bounds of kinship, and, like the rest, "done in secret"; the whole group of curses, the witnesses note, falls on deeds men contrive to hide, that none think to escape by concealment; the law thus teaches that God's curse pursues the hidden uncleanness as surely as the open, his eye in every dark place, and that the secret sinner stands as accursed before him as the public (cf. Eccl. 12:14, God shall bring every secret thing into judgment).

So the witnesses make the verse cursed the secret incest: The witnesses note the his mother in law: the last of the incest-curses. The witnesses note the a breach of kinship: the bounds broken. The witnesses note the done in secret: hidden from men. The witnesses note the no escape by concealment: every secret thing judged (Eccl. 12:14). The expositors observe the his mother in law: the last of the incest-curses. The expositors observe the a breach of kinship: the bounds broken. The expositors observe the done in secret: hidden from men. The expositors observe the no escape by concealment: every secret thing judged (Eccl. 12:14). The believer is taught to mark the his mother in law, the a breach of kinship, the done in secret, the no escape by concealment, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who lies with his mother-in-law — completing the secret sins of the flesh, hidden from men but cursed before God, who brings every secret thing into judgment (Eccl. 12:14).
24:
"Cursed be he that smiteth his neighbour secretly. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the curse on secret violence — the hidden murderer, or the secret slanderer. The witnesses note that Gill notes the smiting and killing of a neighbour secretly, the murder unknown, the curse of God following; some take it of smiting with the tongue, secret backbiting and slander, "the third tongue" of the Targum. The witnesses note that all the people said, Amen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the smiteth secretly — the hidden murderer; the or by the tongue — secret slander and backbiting; the escapes man's justice — but not God's; the the hidden injury avenged — privy slander cut off (Ps. 101:5).

The witnesses agree the curse falls on hidden harm done to a neighbour, whether by stealthy violence or by the secret tongue: to "smite his neighbour secretly" — the unknown murderer whose deed escapes man's justice, or, as some read, the backbiter who smites by slander unseen — is pursued by the curse of God, who sees what men cannot; the law thus reaches the wrongs that hide from human courts, the secret blow and the secret lie alike; it teaches that God avenges the hidden injury, and that the stealthy harming of a neighbour, by hand or tongue, is accursed before him who judges in secret (cf. Ps. 101:5, whoso privily slandereth his neighbour, him will I cut off).

So the witnesses make the verse cursed the secret blow: The witnesses note the smiteth secretly: the hidden murderer. The witnesses note the or by the tongue: secret slander and backbiting. The witnesses note the escapes man's justice: but not God's. The witnesses note the the hidden injury avenged: privy slander cut off (Ps. 101:5). The expositors observe the smiteth secretly: the hidden murderer. The expositors observe the or by the tongue: secret slander and backbiting. The expositors observe the escapes man's justice: but not God's. The expositors observe the the hidden injury avenged: privy slander cut off (Ps. 101:5). The believer is taught to mark the smiteth secretly, the or by the tongue, the escapes man's justice, the the hidden injury avenged, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who smites his neighbour secretly — the hidden blow or the secret slander pursued by God's curse, the wrong that escapes man's courts avenged by him who judges in secret (Ps. 101:5).
25:
"Cursed be he that taketh reward to slay an innocent person. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the curse on him who takes a bribe to destroy the innocent — the hired assassin or the corrupt witness. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the assassin who murders privately for hire, or the judge who takes a bribe to condemn the innocent, or, as he thinks, a false witness who swears away a man's life. The witnesses note that all the people said, Amen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the taketh reward — a bribe for blood; the to slay the innocent — the hired assassin or false witness; the greed and bloodshed — the basest wickedness; the innocent blood avenged — the LORD the defence of the innocent (Ps. 94:21-22).

The witnesses agree the curse falls on the basest selling of justice and life for money: to "take reward to slay an innocent person" — whether as a hired murderer, a bribed judge, or, as Gill prefers, a false witness who perjures away a man's life — is a crowning wickedness, joining greed to bloodshed; the law brands as cursed the trafficking in innocent blood, the perversion of justice for gain; it teaches that God peculiarly abhors the shedding of innocent blood and the corruption of judgment by bribery, "they... slay the innocent... but the LORD is my defence" (Ps. 94:21-22), the hired destroyer accursed before him.

So the witnesses make the verse cursed who sells blood: The witnesses note the taketh reward: a bribe for blood. The witnesses note the to slay the innocent: the hired assassin or false witness. The witnesses note the greed and bloodshed: the basest wickedness. The witnesses note the innocent blood avenged: the LORD the defence of the innocent (Ps. 94:21-22). The expositors observe the taketh reward: a bribe for blood. The expositors observe the to slay the innocent: the hired assassin or false witness. The expositors observe the greed and bloodshed: the basest wickedness. The expositors observe the innocent blood avenged: the LORD the defence of the innocent (Ps. 94:21-22). The believer is taught to mark the taketh reward, the to slay the innocent, the greed and bloodshed, the innocent blood avenged, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who takes reward to slay the innocent — the trafficking in innocent blood, greed joined to bloodshed, accursed before God who abhors the corruption of justice and is the defence of the innocent (Ps. 94:21-22).
26:
"Cursed be he that confirmeth not all the words of this law to do them. And all the people shall say, Amen." The witnesses note the final, all-embracing curse — on whoever does not keep the whole law perfectly. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is whoever does not perfectly and constantly perform all the law requires; for the law requires perfect and constant obedience, and curses upon failure — the apostle so producing this passage. The witnesses note that all the people said, Amen. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the confirmeth not all — every failure cursed; the to do them — perfect, constant obedience required; the all under the curse — no man keeping it perfectly (Gal. 3:10); the made a curse for us — christ redeeming from it (Gal. 3:13).

The witnesses agree the twelfth curse is the great and universal one, the key to all the rest: "cursed be he that confirmeth not all the words of this law to do them" — not this or that sin only, but every failure to keep the whole law perfectly and constantly falls under the curse; the apostle quotes this very verse to prove that "as many as are of the works of the law are under the curse" (Gal. 3:10), for no man keeps it perfectly; the curse thus shuts up all under sin and drives the soul to Christ, "who hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us" (Gal. 3:13) — the law's curse the very thing that sends the sinner to the cross.

So the witnesses make the verse the universal curse: The witnesses note the confirmeth not all: every failure cursed. The witnesses note the to do them: perfect, constant obedience required. The witnesses note the all under the curse: no man keeping it perfectly (Gal. 3:10). The witnesses note the made a curse for us: Christ redeeming from it (Gal. 3:13). The expositors observe the confirmeth not all: every failure cursed. The expositors observe the to do them: perfect, constant obedience required. The expositors observe the all under the curse: no man keeping it perfectly (Gal. 3:10). The expositors observe the made a curse for us: Christ redeeming from it (Gal. 3:13). The believer is taught to mark the confirmeth not all, the to do them, the all under the curse, the made a curse for us, and to flee the curse to Christ who bore it. The verse thus records that cursed is he who keeps not all the law perfectly — the universal curse shutting up all under sin, driving the soul to Christ, who was made a curse for us to redeem us from the curse of the law (Gal. 3:10, 13).
